title: COVID-19 Pandemic
description: Beginning in late 2019 and escalating globally through 2020, COVID-19 forced governments to make simultaneous public-health and macroeconomic decisions under extreme uncertainty. Policymakers had to balance mobility restrictions, testing and treatment capacity, fiscal support, and vaccine strategy while preserving social trust and institutional legitimacy.

questions.text: First international cases emerge across East Asia. You must decide the initial containment posture.
questions.fun_fact: On March 11, 2020, the World Health Organization declared COVID-19 a global pandemic.

options.text: Prioritize PPE procurement while keeping borders and mobility mostly unchanged.
options.feedback: Normalcy persists briefly, but disease readiness is perceived as too passive once cases rise.

options.text: Enforce 14-day quarantine for inbound travelers and suspend non-essential international travel.
options.feedback: Spread slows initially, but response infrastructure remains underdeveloped for later waves.

options.text: Restrict travel from high-risk regions and deploy scientific teams for rapid virus intelligence.
options.feedback: Early research gains improve medium-term strategy, though near-term containment remains uneven.

options.text: Pair targeted travel restrictions with immediate investment in national testing infrastructure.
options.feedback: Testing and traceability improve quickly, strengthening decisions as community spread begins.

questions.text: Transmission accelerates sharply. You need a social-policy package to reduce spread.
questions.fun_fact: Non-pharmaceutical interventions were the primary response before vaccine deployment scaled globally.

options.text: Issue optional distancing and masking guidance while keeping normal operations.
options.feedback: Economic continuity is higher short-term, but mortality and system pressure rise quickly.

options.text: Use quarantine enforcement for exposed individuals while recommending masks and distancing.
options.feedback: Outcomes improve versus no controls, but inconsistent compliance weakens containment.

options.text: Impose strict city-wide lockdowns with digital enforcement and broad mobility suppression.
options.feedback: Transmission drops, but social backlash and economic contraction intensify.

options.text: Close schools and non-essential businesses, enforce masking, and preserve essential movement.
options.feedback: Transmission is reduced with more manageable social legitimacy than maximal lockdowns.

questions.text: The health crisis is now a jobs and liquidity crisis. How do you protect households and firms?
questions.fun_fact: Global GDP contracted sharply in 2020 while emergency fiscal measures reached historic levels in many countries.

options.text: Prioritize low-rate loans and business incentives to preserve corporate balance sheets.
options.feedback: Fiscal costs are controlled, but household demand recovers slowly and inequality widens.

options.text: Target hardest-hit sectors and subsidize reduced working hours.
options.feedback: Layoffs moderate, but patchwork support leaves parts of the economy exposed.

options.text: Deploy broad household stimulus, expanded unemployment benefits, and large-scale liquidity operations.
options.feedback: Demand rebounds quickly, though inflation and debt pressures rise.

options.text: Combine strong wage replacement, sector support, self-employed grants, and quantitative easing.
options.feedback: The social floor holds and recovery broadens, but long-term fiscal discipline becomes harder.

questions.text: Vaccines are available. You must decide access, funding, and public-health enforcement.
questions.fun_fact: Leading vaccine candidates reported approximately 95% efficacy in late-stage 2020 trials.

options.text: Secure early supply through premium contracts with significant out-of-pocket costs.
options.feedback: Access begins quickly, but inequity undermines overall health outcomes.

options.text: Publicly fund high-risk groups first; allow paid access outside priority groups.
options.feedback: High-risk protection improves, but mixed access slows broad immunity.

options.text: Subsidize free vaccination for all citizens.
options.feedback: Coverage rises strongly and trust improves, though logistics still limit speed.

options.text: Provide universally free vaccination with narrow mandates for critical settings.
options.feedback: Protection reaches population scale fastest, with manageable but real freedom concerns.

questions.text: Cases are moderating but risks remain. How do you phase out emergency policy without causing relapse?
questions.fun_fact: Many economies faced simultaneous goals: restoring mobility, sustaining growth, and preventing renewed health shocks.

options.text: Remove major fiscal and monetary supports once cases fall below a threshold.
options.feedback: Policy space improves, but fragile firms and households face abrupt adjustment pressure.

options.text: Reopen schools and businesses at mass vaccination milestones.
options.feedback: Normalcy returns quickly, but resilience to new variants remains uneven.

options.text: Maintain closures and strong precautions until formal pandemic-end declaration.
options.feedback: Health risk declines, but delayed reopening strains education, livelihoods, and social cohesion.

options.text: Phase reopening using both case thresholds and vaccination coverage while tapering supports gradually.
options.feedback: The transition is steadier, balancing health protection with institutional and economic recovery.

result_bands.title: Fragile Outcome
result_bands.summary: Cases remain elevated, vaccination is uneven, and economic stability is vulnerable to recurring disruptions.

result_bands.title: Strained but Recoverable
result_bands.summary: Core systems hold and unemployment is contained, but equity and long-term resilience remain unsettled.

result_bands.title: Stable with Trade-offs
result_bands.summary: Vaccination and policy support stabilize the system, though inflation, debt, and sector distortions persist.

result_bands.title: Strategic Advantage
result_bands.summary: Public-health control and adaptive policy sequencing reinforce trust, recovery, and institutional durability.
